Data Validation

Complete documentation for data validation in Aspose.Cells FOSS for .NET

â†?Back to Index

Data Validation Overview

Data validation allows you to control what users can enter into cells in your worksheets. You can restrict input to specific values, ranges, data types, or custom formulas, and provide error messages when users enter invalid data.

Key Validation Features:
  • Restrict cell input to specific data types (whole numbers, decimals, dates, times, text length, etc.)
  • Create in-cell dropdown lists
  • Set value ranges (minimum, maximum, between, not between)
  • Use custom formulas for complex validation rules
  • Display input prompts to guide users
  • Show error messages when validation fails
  • Choose between warning or error styles

Validation Types

Available Validation Types
// Whole number validation
var validation = worksheet.Validations.Add(ValidationType.WholeNumber);

// Decimal validation
var validation = worksheet.Validations.Add(ValidationType.Decimal);

// List validation (dropdown)
var validation = worksheet.Validations.Add(ValidationType.List);

// Date validation
var validation = worksheet.Validations.Add(ValidationType.Date);

// Time validation
var validation = worksheet.Validations.Add(ValidationType.Time);

// Text length validation
var validation = worksheet.Validations.Add(ValidationType.TextLength);

// Custom formula validation
var validation = worksheet.Validations.Add(ValidationType.Custom);

// Any value (no restriction)
var validation = worksheet.Validations.Add(ValidationType.AnyValue);

Creating Data Validations

Whole Number Validation
// Create whole number validation for range A1:A10
var validation = worksheet.Validations.Add(ValidationType.WholeNumber);
validation.Operator = OperatorType.Between;
validation.Formula1 = "1";
validation.Formula2 = "100";
validation.ErrorMessage = "Please enter a whole number between 1 and 100";
validation.ErrorTitle = "Invalid Input";
validation.ShowError = true;
validation.AlertStyle = ValidationAlertType.Stop;

// Apply validation to range
validation.AreaList.Add(worksheet.Cells.CreateRange(0, 0, 10, 1));  // A1:A10
Decimal Validation
// Create decimal validation
var validation = worksheet.Validations.Add(ValidationType.Decimal);
validation.Operator = OperatorType.GreaterThanOrEqual;
validation.Formula1 = "0.01";
validation.ErrorMessage = "Please enter a value greater than or equal to 0.01";
validation.ShowError = true;
validation.AlertStyle = ValidationAlertType.Stop;

// Apply to cell B1
validation.AreaList.Add(worksheet.Cells.CreateRange(0, 1, 1, 1));  // B1
Date Validation
// Create date validation for future dates only
var validation = worksheet.Validations.Add(ValidationType.Date);
validation.Operator = OperatorType.GreaterThan;
validation.Formula1 = "TODAY()";
validation.ErrorMessage = "Please enter a future date";
validation.ShowError = true;
validation.AlertStyle = ValidationAlertType.Warning;

// Apply to date entry cells
validation.AreaList.Add(worksheet.Cells.CreateRange(2, 0, 10, 1));  // A3:A12
Time Validation
// Create time validation for business hours (9 AM - 5 PM)
var validation = worksheet.Validations.Add(ValidationType.Time);
validation.Operator = OperatorType.Between;
validation.Formula1 = "9:00";
validation.Formula2 = "17:00";
validation.ErrorMessage = "Please enter a time between 9:00 AM and 5:00 PM";
validation.ShowError = true;

// Apply to time entry cells
validation.AreaList.Add(worksheet.Cells.CreateRange(3, 2, 10, 1));  // C4:C13
Text Length Validation
// Create text length validation (must be between 3 and 50 characters)
var validation = worksheet.Validations.Add(ValidationType.TextLength);
validation.Operator = OperatorType.Between;
validation.Formula1 = "3";
validation.Formula2 = "50";
validation.ErrorMessage = "Text must be between 3 and 50 characters";
validation.ShowError = true;
validation.AlertStyle = ValidationAlertType.Stop;

// Apply to description cells
validation.AreaList.Add(worksheet.Cells.CreateRange(4, 1, 20, 1));  // B5:B24

List Validation (Dropdown)

Static List
// Create dropdown list with static values
var validation = worksheet.Validations.Add(ValidationType.List);
validation.Formula1 = "Apple,Banana,Orange,Grape,Mango";
validation.ErrorMessage = "Please select a valid fruit from the list";
validation.ShowError = true;

// Apply to cell A1
validation.AreaList.Add(worksheet.Cells.CreateRange(0, 0, 1, 1));

// Add input prompt
validation.InputMessage = "Select a fruit from the dropdown list";
validation.InputTitle = "Fruit Selection";
validation.ShowInput = true;
Dynamic List (Cell Range)
// First, populate a list of options in a range
string[] departments = { "HR", "Finance", "IT", "Sales", "Marketing", "Operations" };
for (int i = 0; i < departments.Length; i++)
{
    worksheet.Cells[i + 20, 0].PutValue(departments[i]);
}

// Create dropdown list referencing the range
var validation = worksheet.Validations.Add(ValidationType.List);
validation.Formula1 = "=$A$20:$A$25";  // Reference to department list
validation.ErrorMessage = "Please select a valid department";
validation.ShowError = true;

// Apply to department selection cells
validation.AreaList.Add(worksheet.Cells.CreateRange(1, 0, 10, 1));  // A2:A11
Dynamic List (Named Range)
// Create a named range for the list
workbook.DefinedNames.Add("DepartmentList", "Sheet1!$A$20:$A$25");

// Use named range in validation
var validation = worksheet.Validations.Add(ValidationType.List);
validation.Formula1 = "DepartmentList";
validation.ErrorMessage = "Please select a department";
validation.ShowInput = true;
validation.InputMessage = "Select from available departments";

// Apply to cells
validation.AreaList.Add(worksheet.Cells.CreateRange(2, 1, 15, 1));  // B3:B17

Validation Operators

Comparison Operators
// Between (requires Formula1 and Formula2)
validation.Operator = OperatorType.Between;

// Not between (requires Formula1 and Formula2)
validation.Operator = OperatorType.NotBetween;

// Equal to
validation.Operator = OperatorType.Equal;

// Not equal to
validation.Operator = OperatorType.NotEqual;

// Greater than
validation.Operator = OperatorType.GreaterThan;

// Less than
validation.Operator = OperatorType.LessThan;

// Greater than or equal to
validation.Operator = OperatorType.GreaterThanOrEqual;

// Less than or equal to
validation.Operator = OperatorType.LessThanOrEqual;

Custom Formula Validation

Formula-Based Rules
// Create custom validation using formula
var validation = worksheet.Validations.Add(ValidationType.Custom);

// Ensure cell value is even number
validation.Formula1 = "=MOD(A1, 2) = 0";
validation.ErrorMessage = "Please enter an even number";
validation.ShowError = true;
validation.AlertStyle = ValidationAlertType.Stop;
validation.AreaList.Add(worksheet.Cells.CreateRange(0, 0, 10, 1));

// Ensure cell value is not empty
var validation2 = worksheet.Validations.Add(ValidationType.Custom);
validation2.Formula1 = "=LEN(TRIM(A1)) > 0";
validation2.ErrorMessage = "This field cannot be empty";
validation2.ShowError = true;
validation2.AlertStyle = ValidationAlertType.Stop;
validation2.AreaList.Add(worksheet.Cells.CreateRange(1, 2, 10, 1));

// Ensure email format (simple check for @ symbol)
var validation3 = worksheet.Validations.Add(ValidationType.Custom);
validation3.Formula1 = "=ISNUMBER(FIND(\"@\", A1))";
validation3.ErrorMessage = "Please enter a valid email address";
validation3.ShowError = true;
validation3.AlertStyle = ValidationAlertType.Warning;
validation3.AreaList.Add(worksheet.Cells.CreateRange(2, 0, 20, 1));
Complex Custom Validations
// Validate product code (starts with PRD followed by 5 digits)
var validation = worksheet.Validations.Add(ValidationType.Custom);
validation.Formula1 = "=AND(LEFT(A1,3)=\"PRD\", LEN(A1)=8, ISNUMBER(VALUE(RIGHT(A1,5))))";
validation.ErrorMessage = "Product code must be PRD followed by 5 digits (e.g., PRD12345)";
validation.ShowError = true;
validation.AlertStyle = ValidationAlertType.Stop;
validation.AreaList.Add(worksheet.Cells.CreateRange(0, 0, 50, 1));

// Validate phone number format (###-###-####)
var validation2 = worksheet.Validations.Add(ValidationType.Custom);
validation2.Formula1 = "=AND(LEN(A1)=12, ISNUMBER(VALUE(LEFT(A1,3))), MID(A1,4,1)=\"-\", ISNUMBER(VALUE(MID(A1,5,3))), MID(A1,8,1)=\"-\", ISNUMBER(VALUE(RIGHT(A1,4))))";
validation2.ErrorMessage = "Phone number format must be ###-###-####";
validation2.ShowError = true;
validation2.AlertStyle = ValidationAlertType.Stop;
validation2.AreaList.Add(worksheet.Cells.CreateRange(1, 1, 50, 1));

Error Messages and Alerts

Alert Styles
// Stop - Prevents invalid entry
validation.AlertStyle = ValidationAlertType.Stop;
validation.ErrorMessage = "This value is not allowed";
validation.ErrorTitle = "Error";
validation.ShowError = true;

// Warning - Warns but allows invalid entry
validation.AlertStyle = ValidationAlertType.Warning;
validation.ErrorMessage = "This value may not be appropriate";
validation.ErrorTitle = "Warning";
validation.ShowError = true;

// Information - Provides information but allows invalid entry
validation.AlertStyle = ValidationAlertType.Information;
validation.ErrorMessage = "Note about this value";
validation.ErrorTitle = "Information";
validation.ShowError = true;
Input Messages
// Show input message when cell is selected
validation.ShowInput = true;
validation.InputTitle = "Instructions";
validation.InputMessage = "Please enter a value between 1 and 100";

// Example with dropdown
validation.ShowInput = true;
validation.InputTitle = "Select Option";
validation.InputMessage = "Click the arrow to select from the dropdown list";

// Hide input message
validation.ShowInput = false;

Managing Validations

Accessing Validations
// Access validation collection
var validations = worksheet.Validations;

// Get validation count
int count = validations.Count;

// Access specific validation by index
var firstValidation = validations[0];

// Remove validation from a specific cell
validations.RemoveACell(4, 0);

// Remove validation from an area
validations.RemoveArea(CellArea.CreateCellArea("A5", "A10"));
Check Cell Validation
// Check validation in a specific cell`r`nvar validation = worksheet.Validations.GetValidationInCell(4, 0);`r`n`r`nif (validation != null)
{
    Console.WriteLine("Cell has data validation");
}

Complete Example

Employee Data Entry Form
using Aspose.Cells_FOSS;
using System;

class Program
{
    static void Main()
    {
        var workbook = new Workbook();
        var worksheet = workbook.Worksheets[0];
        var cells = worksheet.Cells;

        // Create header
        cells["A1"].PutValue("Employee Data Entry");
        var headerStyle = cells["A1"].GetStyle();
        headerStyle.Font.Bold = true;
        headerStyle.Font.Size = 14;
        headerStyle.Font.Color = System.Drawing.Color.White;
        headerStyle.BackgroundColor = System.Drawing.Color.FromArgb(0, 51, 153);
        cells["A1"].SetStyle(headerStyle);

        // Create labels
        cells["A3"].PutValue("Employee ID:");
        cells["A4"].PutValue("Full Name:");
        cells["A5"].PutValue("Department:");
        cells["A6"].PutValue("Hire Date:");
        cells["A7"].PutValue("Salary:");
        cells["A8"].PutValue("Email:");
        cells["A9"].PutValue("Phone:");

        // Apply label styling
        var labelStyle = workbook.CreateStyle();
        labelStyle.Font.Bold = true;
        for (int row = 3; row <= 9; row++)
        {
            cells[row, 0].SetStyle(labelStyle);
        }

        // Validation for Employee ID (must be EMP followed by 4 digits)
        var empIdValidation = worksheet.Validations.Add(ValidationType.Custom);
        empIdValidation.Formula1 = "=AND(LEFT(B3,3)=\"EMP\", LEN(B3)=7, ISNUMBER(VALUE(RIGHT(B3,4))))";
        empIdValidation.ErrorMessage = "Employee ID must be EMP followed by 4 digits (e.g., EMP1234)";
        empIdValidation.ErrorTitle = "Invalid Employee ID";
        empIdValidation.ShowInput = true;
        empIdValidation.InputMessage = "Enter employee ID (format: EMP####)";
        empIdValidation.ShowError = true;
        empIdValidation.AlertStyle = ValidationAlertType.Stop;
        empIdValidation.AreaList.Add(cells.CreateRange(2, 1, 1, 1));

        // Validation for Department (dropdown list)
        var deptValidation = worksheet.Validations.Add(ValidationType.List);
        deptValidation.Formula1 = "HR,Finance,IT,Sales,Marketing,Operations,Engineering";
        deptValidation.ErrorMessage = "Please select a valid department";
        deptValidation.ErrorTitle = "Invalid Department";
        deptValidation.ShowInput = true;
        deptValidation.InputMessage = "Select department from dropdown";
        deptValidation.ShowError = true;
        deptValidation.AlertStyle = ValidationAlertType.Stop;
        deptValidation.AreaList.Add(cells.CreateRange(4, 1, 1, 1));

        // Validation for Hire Date (must be future date)
        var dateValidation = worksheet.Validations.Add(ValidationType.Date);
        dateValidation.Operator = OperatorType.GreaterThan;
        dateValidation.Formula1 = "TODAY()";
        dateValidation.ErrorMessage = "Hire date must be a future date";
        dateValidation.ErrorTitle = "Invalid Date";
        dateValidation.ShowInput = true;
        dateValidation.InputMessage = "Enter future hire date";
        dateValidation.ShowError = true;
        dateValidation.AlertStyle = ValidationAlertType.Warning;
        dateValidation.AreaList.Add(cells.CreateRange(5, 1, 1, 1));

        // Validation for Salary (between 30000 and 200000)
        var salaryValidation = worksheet.Validations.Add(ValidationType.WholeNumber);
        salaryValidation.Operator = OperatorType.Between;
        salaryValidation.Formula1 = "30000";
        salaryValidation.Formula2 = "200000";
        salaryValidation.ErrorMessage = "Salary must be between $30,000 and $200,000";
        salaryValidation.ErrorTitle = "Invalid Salary";
        salaryValidation.ShowInput = true;
        salaryValidation.InputMessage = "Enter annual salary ($30,000 - $200,000)";
        salaryValidation.ShowError = true;
        salaryValidation.AlertStyle = ValidationAlertType.Stop;
        salaryValidation.AreaList.Add(cells.CreateRange(6, 1, 1, 1));

        // Validation for Email (must contain @)
        var emailValidation = worksheet.Validations.Add(ValidationType.Custom);
        emailValidation.Formula1 = "=AND(ISNUMBER(FIND(\"@\", B8)), ISNUMBER(FIND(\".\", B8)), LEN(B8) > 5)";
        emailValidation.ErrorMessage = "Please enter a valid email address";
        emailValidation.ErrorTitle = "Invalid Email";
        emailValidation.ShowInput = true;
        emailValidation.InputMessage = "Enter email address (must contain @ and .)";
        emailValidation.ShowError = true;
        emailValidation.AlertStyle = ValidationAlertType.Warning;
        emailValidation.AreaList.Add(cells.CreateRange(7, 1, 1, 1));

        // Validation for Phone (format ###-###-####)
        var phoneValidation = worksheet.Validations.Add(ValidationType.Custom);
        phoneValidation.Formula1 = "=AND(LEN(B9)=12, ISNUMBER(VALUE(LEFT(B9,3))), MID(B9,4,1)=\"-\", ISNUMBER(VALUE(MID(B9,5,3))), MID(B9,8,1)=\"-\", ISNUMBER(VALUE(RIGHT(B9,4))))";
        phoneValidation.ErrorMessage = "Phone number must be in format ###-###-####";
        phoneValidation.ErrorTitle = "Invalid Phone";
        phoneValidation.ShowInput = true;
        phoneValidation.InputMessage = "Enter phone number (format: ###-###-####)";
        phoneValidation.ShowError = true;
        phoneValidation.AlertStyle = ValidationAlertType.Stop;
        phoneValidation.AreaList.Add(cells.CreateRange(8, 1, 1, 1));

        // Auto-fit columns
        worksheet.AutoFitColumns();

        // Save workbook
        workbook.Save("employee-entry-form.xlsx");
        Console.WriteLine("Employee data entry form created with validations!");
    }
}
Product Inventory System
using Aspose.Cells_FOSS;
using System;

class Program
{
    static void Main()
    {
        var workbook = new Workbook();
        var worksheet = workbook.Worksheets[0];
        var cells = worksheet.Cells;

        // Create inventory header
        cells["A1"].PutValue("Product Inventory");
        cells["B1"].PutValue("Quantity");
        cells["C1"].PutValue("Price");
        cells["D1"].PutValue("Total");

        // Create department dropdown (static list)
        var categoryValidation = worksheet.Validations.Add(ValidationType.List);
        categoryValidation.Formula1 = "Electronics,Clothing,Home,Garden,Books,Sports";
        categoryValidation.ShowInput = true;
        categoryValidation.InputMessage = "Select product category";
        categoryValidation.ErrorMessage = "Please select a valid category";
        categoryValidation.ShowError = true;
        categoryValidation.AlertStyle = ValidationAlertType.Stop;

        // Create quantity validation (positive whole numbers)
        var quantityValidation = worksheet.Validations.Add(ValidationType.WholeNumber);
        quantityValidation.Operator = OperatorType.GreaterThan;
        quantityValidation.Formula1 = "0";
        quantityValidation.ShowInput = true;
        quantityValidation.InputMessage = "Enter quantity (must be positive)";
        quantityValidation.ErrorMessage = "Quantity must be a positive number";
        quantityValidation.ShowError = true;
        quantityValidation.AlertStyle = ValidationAlertType.Stop;

        // Create price validation (positive decimals)
        var priceValidation = worksheet.Validations.Add(ValidationType.Decimal);
        priceValidation.Operator = OperatorType.GreaterThan;
        priceValidation.Formula1 = "0";
        priceValidation.ShowInput = true;
        priceValidation.InputMessage = "Enter unit price (must be positive)";
        priceValidation.ErrorMessage = "Price must be a positive number";
        priceValidation.ShowError = true;
        priceValidation.AlertStyle = ValidationAlertType.Stop;

        // Apply validations to data entry rows
        for (int row = 2; row <= 20; row++)
        {
            // Category dropdown
            categoryValidation.AreaList.Add(cells.CreateRange(row - 1, 0, 1, 1));

            // Quantity validation
            quantityValidation.AreaList.Add(cells.CreateRange(row - 1, 1, 1, 1));

            // Price validation
            priceValidation.AreaList.Add(cells.CreateRange(row - 1, 2, 1, 1));

            // Add total formula
            cells[row - 1, 3].Formula = $"=B{row}*C{row}";
        }

        // Save workbook
        workbook.Save("product-inventory.xlsx");
        Console.WriteLine("Product inventory system with validations created!");
    }
}

Important Notes

  • Validation Persistence: Data validation rules are saved with the workbook and work when the file is opened in Excel.
  • Cell References: In custom formulas, use cell references relative to the validated cell.
  • List Values: List values can be comma-separated strings or cell range references.
  • Alert Styles: Use Stop for strict validation, Warning for soft warnings, and Information for helpful messages.
  • Performance: Too many validations can impact performance; use only where necessary.
  • Named Ranges: Use named ranges for dynamic lists to make validation rules more maintainable.
  • Circular References: Avoid circular references in custom validation formulas.

Related Documentation

  • Worksheet Operations - Managing worksheets and cells
  • Conditional Formatting - Visual formatting based on cell values
  • Formulas - Working with formulas in validations
  • Styling - Cell formatting for data entry forms
